Fitch Affirms Ingredion Incorporated's IDR at 'BBB'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Toronto-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Ingredion Incorporated's (Ingredion) ratings, including the Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'BBB' and Short-Term IDR at 'F2'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

Ingredion's ratings reflect its globally diverse product portfolio and stable underlying business model. The company has benefited from various strategic investments over the years, combined with operational efficiencies, cost-saving initiatives, and a proactive transformation of its portfolio to address evolving market trends. While Ingredion remains primarily focused on food and industrial starches and sweeteners, it has steadily expanded into higher-growth, value-added ingredient solution categories.

Fitch expects these actions to support reduced earnings volatility, an improved margin profile, and more predictable long-term earnings growth. Fitch also anticipates that Ingredion will maintain strong financial discipline, with balanced capital allocation around growth investments, mergers and acquisitions (M&A), and shareholder returns. This together is expected to support the company's long-term net leverage target in the low-2x range.

Key Rating Drivers

Portfolio Transformation Supports Margins: Ingredion has diversified beyond basic sweeteners and starches over the past several years through investments, including bolt-on acquisitions. The company has been increasing its focus on higher growth, and higher-margin value-added areas such as customized formulations, clean label, sugar reduction, and protein and fiber fortification amongst others, offsetting commodity driven volatility in the portfolio and modest industry declines in high fructose corn syrup.

This portfolio transformation, along with efficiency initiatives and portfolio rationalizations supports reduced earnings volatility, and higher and more sustainable EBITDA margins over the longer term. EBITDA margins trended at 17% in 2024, up from around 15% in 2023 and the low-teens range in 2021-2022. Fitch expects margins to be close to 18% in 2025, and sustain in the high teens range over the medium term, assuming relatively stable corn prices, with EBITDA maintained around $1.3 billion absent material M&A.

Investor Day Targets: Ingredion's 2025-2027 target, announced at its recent September 2025 investor day, include overall net sales growth of 2%-4%, adjusted operating income growth of 5%-7%, and adjusted earnings per share (EPS) growth of 7%-9%. The company's Texture & Healthful Solutions segment ($2.4 billion of $7.4 billion of 2024 net revenues), which includes specialty starch-based and clean-label texturizers, hydrocolloids and customized formulations, is expected to generate higher topline growth and adjusted gross margin prospects relative to the remainder of the portfolio.

Fitch's forecasts reflect low-single-digit topline declines in 2025, driven predominately by pass-throughs of lower corn pricing. Revenue is forecasted to decline in the low- to mid-single digits in 2026, assuming the partial sale of the company's Pakistan business during 1H26. Topline is projected to grow in the low-single digits thereafter, reflecting continued solid demand for basic sweeteners and starches, as well as increased exposure to other higher-growth product categories such as clean-label and other healthful solutions.

Balanced Capital Allocation, Good FCF: Fitch expects Ingredion's capital allocation over the medium to longer term to focus on organic growth platform investments, M&A and shareholder returns. The company introduced a new share repurchase program of up to 8 million shares over the next three years (around $850 million based on November 2025 average share price). Fitch expects FCF to be around $200 million in 2025, reflecting moderate working capital investments, and is projected to sustain in the $200 million-$300 million range over the medium term.

Low-2x Net Leverage Target: Ingredion has a target to maintain long-term net leverage in the low 2x range despite currently lower leverage levels. Leverage at YE 2024 was at 0.7x on management's net leverage basis and 1.5x on Fitch's gross EBITDA leverage basis, with Fitch's gross measure 0.2x-0.8x higher over the last three years due to differing cash levels. Fitch expects EBITDA leverage to trend in the mid-1x range in 2025 and increase toward the company's leverage target over the medium to longer term, driven by M&A, growth investments, and/or shareholder return initiatives.

Updated Segmentation: The company reorganized its business operations in 2024 alongside its business transformation initiatives to better match market growth opportunities. The new reportable segments include Texture & Healthful Solutions (32% of 2024 revenues), Food & Industrial Ingredients US/Canada (29%), Food & Industrial Ingredients LATAM (33%), and Other (6%). This compares to the company's previous Specialty Ingredients and Core Ingredients portfolio segmentation.

Diversified Global Player: Ingredion's ratings reflect the company's significant scale as a global ingredient solutions provider for the food, beverage, animal nutrition, brewing and industrial end markets. The company has good geographic diversification, with roughly 61% of net sales derived outside the U.S.

Contractual Pass-Through: Many of Ingredion's contracts are akin to tolling arrangements given cost pass-throughs, with the rest being fixed-price contracts adjusted regularly. Contract terms and cycles vary by region. Ingredion also manages corn exposure through hedging and natural hedging from co- product pricing movements, which generally support operating income stability.

Peer Analysis

Ingredion's IDR is in line with International Flavors & Fragrances Inc.'s (IFF) ratings at 'BBB' with a Stable Outlook.

IFF's rating is supported by its product portfolio's resilient demand profile through the cycle. The company serves largely nondiscretionary end markets, including food, health and personal care components, all of which have historically exhibited low cyclicality.

Ingredion's rating is higher than Primary Products Investments LLC (Primary Products; BB/Negative) and Darling Ingredients, Inc. (Darling; BB+/Stable).

Primary Products' rating reflects the company's strong market position in the mature corn-derived products, offset by narrow product diversification and limited scale. The Negative Outlook reflects the company's elevated Fitch- adjusted EBITDA leverage in the mid-4x range, driven by higher debt levels and Fitch's expectation that leverage could remain above 4x over the next 12-18 months.

Darling's rating reflects the company's leading market position as a globally diversified ingredient processor that has benefited from increasing demand for low-carbon fuels, which supports profitability. Fitch's forecast assumes solid demand for renewable diesel, along with favorable biofuel policy, will support structurally higher margins. Fitch projects Darling's medium-term leverage will trend around 3x, compared with around mid-3x in 2025. This will be through a combination of EBITDA growth, increased Diamond Green Diesel (DGD) cash distribution and debt reduction.

Fitch’s Key Rating-Case Assumptions

--Low-single-digit topline declines in 2025, reflecting weaker corn prices, which are passed through to the customer, offset partially by volume growth. Revenue is forecasted to decline in the low to mid-single digits range in 2026 assuming the partial sale the company's Pakistan business during 1H26. Topline is projected to grow in the low single digit range thereafter.

--EBITDA in the low-$1 billion range absent material M&A. EBITDA margins are projected to sustain in the 17%-18% range over the medium term, compared to 17% in 2024 and around 15% in 2023;

-- FCF of around $200 million in 2025, reflecting moderate working capital investments and capex in the low $400 million range. Fitch expects FCF in the $200 million-$300 million range over the medium term, with flat capex spend and nominal dividend increases;

-- EBITDA leverage in the mid-1x vicinity in 2025. Fitch expects leverage would increase towards the company's low 2x net leverage target over the medium to longer term;

RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

-- EBITDA leverage sustained in the upper-2x due to significant debt-financed acquisitions or share repurchases or sustained operating performance that is weaker than expected.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Positive rating actions are not anticipated in the medium term. However, the following developments could result in a positive rating action:

--Increased confidence in management's targeted net sales growth of 2% to 4% and operating income growth of 5% to 7%, with EBITDA trending toward the mid-$1 billion area, combined with a public commitment to sustain EBITDA leverage below 2.0x;

--Average FCF generation in the mid-$200 million vicinity or higher.

Liquidity and Debt Structure

Ingredion had total liquidity of around $3.8 billion as of Sept. 30, 2025. Domestic liquidity of $1.4 billion comprised of $423 million in cash and cash equivalents and full availability on the company's commercial paper (CP) program, which is backed by the company's $1 billion revolving credit agreement due 2030.

Ingredion's international liquidity was $2.4 billion, consisting of $492 million of cash and cash equivalents, $6 million of short-term investments, as well as $1.9 billion of unused foreign operating lines of credit. Ingredion's next maturity is $500 million of notes due October 2026, which Fitch expects will be refinanced.

Based on Fitch's assessment of Ingredion's financial flexibility, financial structure and operating environment, the higher of the two short-term options for the current rating profile has been assigned at 'F2'. Any material weakening in financial flexibility, financial structure or operating environment conditions could lead to the assignment of the lower of the two short-term rating options for the current long-term profile.

Issuer Profile

Ingredion is a global ingredient solutions provider for the food, beverage, animal nutrition, brewing and industrial end markets. The company is primarily focused on food and industrial starches and sweeteners, but has steadily expanded towards higher-growth, value-added ingredient solution categories.

Summary of Financial Adjustments

Fitch adjusted non-recurring costs, stock-based compensation, and fair value of debt to reflect the debt amount payable at maturity.

MACROECONOMIC ASSUMPTIONS AND SECTOR FORECASTS Click here to access Fitch's latest quarterly Global Corporates Sector Forecasts Monitor data file which aggregates key data points used in our credit analysis. Fitch's macroeconomic forecasts, commodity price assumptions, default rate forecasts, sector key performance indicators and sector-level forecasts are among the data items included. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. data.title: The Multi-Sector Validation Shock: Why SMX Became Impossible for Markets to Ignore
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NEW YORK / ACCESS Newswire / December 5, 2025 / Every market moves on information. Sometimes that information arrives slowly. Sometimes it arrives all at once. The surge in attention around SMX is a case of the latter. It reflects not a single headline or isolated breakthrough, but a convergence of recognition across several industries that had been searching for the same solution without realizing it.

Gold markets were the first to surface this shift. For more than a century, authenticity in the bullion world depended on documents because gold loses its origin the moment it is melted. When SMX demonstrated that a bar can retain its identity at the molecular level through smelting, recasting, vault rotation, and transport, the industry saw a structural barrier fall away. That alone would have been significant. The real shock came next.

Rare earth mineral supply chains started identifying the same solution as their missing link. These materials power electric vehicles, wind turbines, robotics, aerospace systems, and national defense technology. Their processing routes are complex and often opaque. Origin is difficult to prove once ores are blended and refined. SMX allowed rare earths to carry identity from the mine to the magnet. A second global industry realized its foundational weakness had been addressed. It didn't stop there.

Infrastructure, Not Fad

ESG systems began to react in the same way. Regulators, auditors, and brands have struggled for years to validate claims about recycled content and material recovery. SMX provided a path for plastics, textiles, chemicals, and industrial feedstocks to retain identity regardless of processing stage. ESG reporting shifted from estimates to evidence, and another sector began recognizing the same technological root as gold and rare earths.

Digital asset builders simply completed the picture. Through the PCT, real-world material performance became a verified digital signal rather than a modeled approximation. Physical activity could finally be expressed digitally in a way markets could trust.

Four industries. Four independent realizations. One underlying capability. The market is not reacting to noise. It is responding to the multi-sector discovery that SMX's platform solves the same structural flaw across systems that previously had nothing in common. Gold needs persistence of identity. Rare earths need provable lineage. ESG needs traceable data. Digital assets need real-world verification.

When multiple industries converge around the same enabling technology in the same time frame, markets reprice abruptly. They are not reacting to speculation. They are reacting to a correction in understanding. What looked like a recycling technology or a metals tool now appears as a unifying infrastructure layer with relevance across global systems.

The interest is there because the recognition is warranted. SMX did not change its story. Industries changed their understanding of it. Markets are now adjusting to that reality.

About SMX

As global businesses face new and complex challenges relating to carbon neutrality and meeting new governmental and regional regulations and standards, SMX is able to offer players along the value chain access to its marking, tracking, measuring and digital platform technology to transition more successfully to a low-carbon economy.

data.title: Baker Hughes Reports Higher U.S. Rig Count — Market Talk
data.description: Total rigs operating in the U.S. rose by 5 to 549 rigs this week, Baker Hughes reported Friday. Oil rigs led the way, with 6 more operating for the week ended Dec. 5 while one less gas rig was operating. However, rigs this week remain lower than they were at this time last year, with the U.S. count at 589 at this time in 2024. The Canadian rig count is up 3 from last week to 191, with Canadian oil rigs up 5 to 126 and gas rigs down 2 to 65. Crude oil futures are up 0.9% to over $60 a barrel. Brent crude rises 1% to almost $64 a barrel. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: The Tale of Two Canadian Employment Indicators — Market Talk
data.description: Canada's more timely, more closely watched employment data sent jaws dropping with a 53,600 jobs gain in November and steep 0.4-percentage-point drop in the unemployment rate. Some economists worry that the labor-force survey, or LFS, results aren't relaying the true story of Canada's labor market, as they don't match with Statistics Canada's less timely payroll data, known as SEPH. The comparisons, indeed, are stark. The rolling LFS 3-month average rose to 60,200 in November. In contrast, the rolling 3-month average for payroll data, covering the July-to-September period, indicate employment shrank by about 5,000. The LFS's six-month and 12-month averages indicate employment increases of 26,000 and 29,000, respectively. The payroll data indicate much softer employment growth over comparable time periods, of 1,500 on average over six months and 1,100 on average over the past year of data. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

data.title: Fitch Affirms JPMCC 2021-410T
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-05 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed one class of J.P. Morgan Chase Commercial Mortgage Securities Trust 2021-410T, Commercial Mortgage Pass-Through Certificates, Series 2021-410T. The Rating Outlook for class A remains Negative.

JPMCC 2021-410T

----A 46652WAA1; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; AA-sf; Rating Outlook Negative

Transaction Summary

The trust certificates represent the beneficial interest in a $565 million whole mortgage loan that is secured by the borrower's fee interest in 410 10th Avenue, New York, NY. The $300 million trust note A-1 and $185 million trust note B are contributed to this transaction. The $80 million pari-passu non-trust non-controlling companion note A-2 is in BMARK 2021-B24.

The whole mortgage loan is structured as a seven-year, interest-only financing with an anticipated repayment date of Jan.1, 2028 and a scheduled final maturity date of March 1, 2032. During the anticipated repayment date (ARD) tail period, any excess cash flow will be used to hyper-amortize the loan. The trust certificates follow a sequential-pay structure.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

Stable Occupancy and Cash Flow: The affirmations reflect generally stable performance assumptions following a downgrade in December 2024. Fitch's assessment of long-term sustainable net cash flow (NCF) remains unchanged from last rating action and reflects limited property leasing activity but improving submarket conditions. The Negative Outlook reflects the potential for a downgrade should NCF and/or market conditions deteriorate beyond Fitch's view of sustainable performance, and with continued limited leasing momentum and lack of property stabilization over the next one to two years. Fitch will continue to monitor both property and submarket leasing traction.

Property occupancy remained at 65% as of the September 2025 rent roll, from 97% in December 2022. NCF and occupancy declined due to the bankruptcy and vacating of First Republic Bank (33% of NRA / 36% of underwritten base rent at issuance). First Republic Bank was seized by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) in May 2023, and the lease at the property was subsequently terminated.

Fitch's assumption of sustainable cash flow remains unchanged at $38.4 million, approximately 13% below Fitch's issuance NCF of $44 million, reflecting the loss of First Republic Bank. The Fitch NCF also assumes a lease up of vacant spaces grossed up to a discounted rate below in-place rents and a sustainable long-term occupancy assumption of 85%. No new office leases have been executed at the property, the space is currently being marketed for rent. WSD Hudson 1 Ventures. executed a retail lease that commenced in April 2025 for 705 sf, the lease term runs through 2040. The tenant is in a free-rent period.

The largest tenant at the property is Amazon.com Services Inc. which leases 335,408 sf (53% of NRA) through 2037. Amazon.com, Inc. (rated AA-/F1+ by Fitch as of June 2023) provides a lease guarantee. Amazon has a one-time termination option effective in May 2032 which is post loan maturity subject to 600 days written notice. Amazon has two, five-year renewal options. According to Costar, Amazon recently announced layoffs that are expected to affect 91 employees at the property.

The second largest tenant is Hudson Yards Construction LLC, a subsidiary of Related Companies (Related), which leases 70,852 sf (11% NRA) through 2045. Related has the right to terminate its lease effective Oct. 31, 2030 with 18 months prior written notice.

High Quality Property in Strong Location: The property is located on Manhattan's far West Side in close proximity to The Shops and Restaurants at Hudson Yards and Manhattan West Plaza. The area has good accessibility with various public transit options within modest walking distance including bus routes, subways and ferry service. The property was built in 1927 and underwent $145.5 million in capital improvements in 2021. It was repositioned from class C to class A.

Experienced Sponsorship and Management: The property was acquired by 601W Companies in 2020, a New York-based private real estate firm that invests in office properties located throughout the United States.

Interest-Only Loan; ARD: The whole mortgage loan is structured as a seven-year, interest only financing with an anticipated repayment date of Jan. 1, 2028 and a scheduled final maturity date of March 1, 2032 with a fixed rate coupon of 2.59%. In its analysis, Fitch applied an upward loan-to-value (LTV) hurdle adjustment due to the sub-3% coupon. During the ARD tail period, the interest rates on all tranches of debt will increase. Any excess cash flow will be used to hyper-amortize the loan. Without additional leasing at the property, Fitch does not expect there to be any excess cash flow available to pay down the loan.

Fitch Leverage: The $565.0 million mortgage whole loan has a Fitch-stressed DSCR and LTV of 0.77x and 114.8%, respectively, and debt of $889 psf.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Downgrades are likely if property NCF, occupancy and/or market conditions deteriorate beyond Fitch's current view of sustainable performance, including a lack of leasing momentum and/or property stabilization.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

An upgrade to the 'AA-sf' rated class A is possible if the property is able to backfill the vacant space at rental rates above Fitch's assumption of $82-psf and/or occupancy is higher than the current assumption of 85%.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

data.title: EU wheat edges towards contract lows as supply pressure weighs
data.description:
European wheat futures fell for a second straight day on Friday, approaching contract lows as abundant global supplies hung over the market.

March wheat (BL2H6), the most-active contract on Paris-based Euronext, settled 0.3% down at 189.25 euros ($220.23) per metric ton, edging back towards the contract low of 186.75 euros struck on Monday.

Chicago wheat also eased, with grain markets lacking impetus ahead of a widely followed U.S. Department of Agriculture world crop report next Tuesday.

Statistics Canada's bigger-than-expected estimate of the country's wheat crop on Thursday reinforced expectations of hefty supplies across major exporting countries, after recent upward revisions to forecasts in Argentina and Australia.

"It's very subdued before the USDA report, though the market is anticipating that the USDA will factor in the StatsCan estimates," a futures dealer said.

The United Nations' Food and Agriculture Organization on Friday increased its forecast of world cereal production and stocks this season to record highs.

A favorable start to the growing season in Europe added to supply pressure.

Nearly all French soft wheat crops remained in good condition ahead of winter, farm office FranceAgriMer said on Friday.

In oilseeds, front-month February rapeseed (COMG6) on Euronext ended 0.4% higher at 476.50 euros a ton, recovering from a six-week low on Thursday when StatsCan also pegged the country's canola (rapeseed) crop above market expectations.

($1 = 0.8593 euros)

data.title: Parsons Loss of $12.5B FAA Bid Clouds Future Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Parsons lost its bid for a $12.5 billion contract to help the FAA build a new air traffic control system, which throws a wrench in investors' expectations for the contractor's future, Raymond James analysts led by Brian Gesuale say. The analysts downgrade the stock to market perform from strong buy, and shares slide 21%. Not winning the contract creates questions about what will drive growth in the near-term for Parsons, Gesuale says. He believes Parsons has potential growth opportunities, such as Trump's Golden Dome initiative or border security efforts, but those won't benefit the company in the near-term because it hasn't yet secured big contracts. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: California to Issue $19.2 Million in Revenue Bonds for Clinics, Offices
data.description:
By Chris Wack

The California Municipal Finance Authority is offering $19.2 million in revenue bonds to finance the purchase of two buildings to be used as a clinic and administrative offices for Gracelight Community Health.

The bonds would be sold in two tranches, according to documents on MuniOS.

The authority is selling $19.1 million in Series 2025 A bonds, and $175,000 in Series 2025 B federally taxable bonds.

The Series 2025 A bonds mature from June 1, 2026, through 2035. The Series 2025 B bonds mature in 2026. Interest on the bonds will be payable beginning June 1, 2026, and on each subsequent June 1 and Dec. 1.

Proceeds would be used to buy, construct, furnish and equip leasehold improvements within two different buildings in Los Angeles to use as clinics, and a building in Los Angeles for use as administrative offices.

The California Municipal Finance Authority sells bonds and other obligations in order to support economic development, job creation and social programs throughout California.

Piper Sandler is listed as the offering's manager.

The bonds were rated BBB+ by Fitch Ratings.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: The Most Important Industry Isn't AI. It's Healthcare. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Alí R. Bustamante

About the author: Ali R. Bustamante is a professor of practice at the University of New Orleans Department of Economics and Finance.

Scan the latest market commentary and you will hear a familiar refrain: Artificial intelligence is propping up the U.S. economy. Analysts see soaring share prices for Nvidia, Meta Platforms, and Microsoft, along with a wave of data center construction, and conclude that America's economic resilience rests on AI's shoulders.

Confusing the stock market with the real economy is the oldest analytical mistake in finance. AI is propping up the stock market. But healthcare is propping up the economy.

The difference is unmistakable when you look at how these two industries behave in the labor market. Healthcare is the only major sector whose share of total U.S. employment rose in every recession and under nearly every macroeconomic condition of the past 25 years. It has never posted a sustained decline — not during the 2001 economic downturn, not during the 2008-09 global financial crisis, and not during the Covid-19 pandemic-related recession of 2020.

Meanwhile, the much-celebrated AI boom is barely visible in labor-market data. Information sector employment — the broadest proxy for tech — shrank from 2.7% to 1.8% as a share of total jobs during the past 25 years. At the same time, tech firms' equity valuations have skyrocketed.

Employment data should force investors to rethink what is actually sustaining the economy. Healthcare and social assistance employ more than 20 million Americans. The Bureau of Labor Statistics projects it to account for about 38% of all new U.S. jobs over the next decade, far outpacing tech, manufacturing, and construction combined.

The data tell a clear story of steady growth. In 2000, healthcare accounted for just over 8.2% of all nonfarm jobs. By 2025, it had climbed to 11.4%. Crucially, this rise has rarely reversed. No other major sector is close to its level of stability.

Professional and business services, manufacturing, construction, information, retail, accommodation and food services all contracted during downturns. Healthcare didn't just avoid decline. It expanded, month after month, at the exact moment the rest of the economy was breaking. Some of this reflects disproportionate job losses in lower-wage sectors during economic downturns, but it also reflects the essential nature of healthcare. Demand for medical care doesn't disappear in a recession; it intensifies. Economic stress worsens short and long-term health outcomes. It drives up chronic illness, emergency care, and behavioral health needs, forcing hospitals and clinics to staff up precisely when other employers are cutting back.

And healthcare jobs are strong multipliers at the local level. Hospitals and ambulatory care providers support employment in transportation companies, food vendors, educational programs, janitorial services, construction firms, and biomedical suppliers. When a hospital expands, the surrounding economy expands with it. When a hospital closes, the economy immediately contracts. No AI firm has that kind of footprint in Baton Rouge, Des Moines, or Fresno.

This brings us to the real danger on the horizon. The U.S. economy is leaning on a sector whose future stability isn't guaranteed. It is policy-dependent. Unlike AI, whose trajectory is tied to capital markets, investor sentiment, and technological progress, healthcare's stability depends directly on federal and state policy.

Three major healthcare supports are at risk. The enhanced Affordable Care Act subsidies, expanded under the American Rescue Plan, are scheduled to expire at the end of the year. If they lapse, premiums for millions could jump by hundreds of dollars a month. Millions could lose coverage entirely. Rising uninsured rates increase uncompensated-care burdens for hospitals, strain budgets, slow hiring, and weaken regional growth.

In addition, Medicaid and Children's Health Insurance Program enrollment has already fallen sharply as states unwind Covid-19-era continuous-coverage rules. From March 2023 to July 2025, more than 17 million people lost coverage. Hospitals can't absorb that level of uncompensated care without cutting services or closing outright. More than 700 rural hospitals are currently at risk of closing. And when a hospital closes, a regional economy loses one of its few recession-proof anchors.

Finally, the healthcare workforce, already strained by shortages of nurses, medical assistants, mental health counselors, and clinical lab workers, faces an uncertain federal funding landscape. President Donald Trump's proposed fiscal year 2026 budget includes over $400 million in cuts to workforce programs that expand the supply of nurses, physicians, behavioral health specialists, and other healthcare workers. Without stable investments in training and education, health systems can't staff new units, expand services, or meet rising demand. A recession-proof sector becomes recession sensitive.

If policymakers allow Medicaid funding to erode, ACA subsidies to expire, and the health workforce pipeline to thin, they will weaken the very sector that has kept the economy stable through every crisis of the 21st century. No amount of AI-driven equity exuberance will be enough to keep the real economy from feeling the shock.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com.

data.title: Largest tech deals of last decade
data.description:
Netflix on Friday agreed to buy Warner Bros Discovery's TV, film studios and streaming division for $72 billion, a deal that would hand control of one of Hollywood's most prized and oldest assets to the streaming pioneer.

Below is a list of the largest U.S. technology deals in the last decade:
